nodes.en: Traditional recipes are documented food-preparation methods embodying regional and cultural identity. They preserve culinary knowledge and connect communities to land, ingredients, and history.
nodes.cs: Tradiční recepty jsou dokumentované způsoby přípravy jídla vtělující regionální a kulturní identitu. Zachovávají kulinární vědomost a spojují komunity se zemí, ingrediencemi a historií.
nodes.brief:
Traditional recipes are documented food-preparation methods — ingredient combinations, techniques, seasonal timing, ritual food for specific occasions — that carry a community's regional and cultural identity in edible form. A recipe preserves not just a procedure but a relationship to land and season: what grows locally, what was historically scarce or abundant, and what techniques developed in response to available fuel, storage, and climate.

This node was split out from the broader [[10.03]] Traditions & Customs specifically because food preparation carries enough distinct documentary needs — precise measurement, ingredient substitution, technique — to merit its own category, the same reasoning that separated [[10.09]] Traditional Art Techniques for craft practice. Unlike a modern recipe optimized for reproducibility in any kitchen, a traditional recipe is often tied to a specific place, utensil, or hearth technology, so preserving it well means recording that context alongside the ingredient list.

Culinary heritage databases attempt exactly this contextual preservation: the [Ark of Taste](https://www.fondazioneslowfood.com/en/ark-of-taste-slow-food/) catalog run by Slow Food documents endangered regional foods and preparation methods worldwide, and UNESCO recognizes several culinary traditions directly under its [Intangible Cultural Heritage](https://ich.unesco.org/) programme.
